issues.issue: Choices cluster early then go silent for a long stretch (choice-3 at scene-6 to choice-4 at scene-8 is fine, but scene-9x to scene-10 to ending is a long linear tail with no choices in the last ~4 minutes of playtime)
issues.impact: Back third of the episode feels like the player is watching an epilogue rather than playing, deflating momentum right before the emotional payoff
issues.fix: Add a small reactive beat or micro-choice in the walking-home scene (e.g., a text from Alex or Mia the player can respond to) before the final identity choice

issues.issue: Choice-3 option C ('side with Mia') is framed as clearly the 'bad' choice with heavy narrative punishment (Alex goes flat, narrator states 'It very clearly is not fine')
issues.impact: Undercuts the stated design goal of not punishing players narratively for choosing differently; makes the choice feel like a trap rather than genuine trade-off, discouraging experimentation on replay
issues.fix: Soften the narrator's editorializing; let Alex's reaction speak for itself without the narrator explicitly telling the player how to feel about their choice

issues.issue: Opening scene is entirely internal monologue/narration with no player choice for over a minute of playtime
issues.impact: Slight delay before player agency kicks in; risk of passive start for an interactive product
issues.fix: Consider a tiny cosmetic first choice (e.g., how Wren reacts to being watched) to establish agency within the first 30 seconds

issues.issue: Choice-1 (confident vs. cautious hallway intro) has minimal downstream narrative impact — both paths funnel into the same scene-4 with only trait deltas differing
issues.impact: First choice feels lower-stakes than later ones, slightly undercutting the promise of 'every small choice matters'
issues.fix: Give the hallway choice a small callback later (e.g., Mia references whether player was the one who introduced themselves boldly) — this is partially done via branchDialogue but could be more prominent in-scene, not just in optional endings

issues.issue: No explicit tutorial for trait system (CONFIDENCE/EMPATHY/ADAPTABILITY) within the episode text itself — reliant on external UI
issues.impact: New players may not understand why choices matter mechanically until they check a stats screen, reducing felt weight of decisions in the moment
issues.fix: Consider a subtle in-fiction acknowledgment (Jordan or internal monologue hinting 'that felt like a confident move') without breaking immersion — already partially present via INTERNAL lines, could be tuned to reinforce trait feedback

issues.issue: Scene-9c (bridge-groups) resolves very quickly and smoothly compared to the risk it represents — Mia's pushback is a single line before total acceptance
issues.impact: The most interesting/branch-defining choice gets the least narrative weight and tension payoff, making the 'best' path feel narratively cheap
issues.fix: Add a beat of visible awkwardness or a small setback before the group warms up, to make the bridge-builder path feel earned rather than instantly rewarded

strengths: Excellent choice variety: binary, ternary, and identity-reflection choices all present within a short episode
Strong three-act structure (setup/complication/crisis/convergence/resolution) that's easy to follow while playing
The group-project cruelty moment (choice-3) is a genuinely well-designed moral pressure point with three distinct, characterful responses
Branching is meaningfully divergent — scenes 5a/5b, 7a/7b/7c, 9a/9b/9c, and 11a/11b/11c create real content forks, not just flavor text
branchDialogue system elegantly reincorporates earlier choices into the ending scenes, rewarding consistency without requiring separate full endings
Trait mapping is well-tuned to choice content — no arbitrary trait assignment, each option's traits match its narrative logic
Good replay incentive via clearly distinct 'Path A/B/C/D' outcomes documented, encouraging players to try different approaches
recommendations: Add one more small choice or interactive beat in the final third to prevent it from feeling like a cutscene
Rebalance scene-7c so choosing to go along with Mia doesn't read as an obviously 'punished' choice
Give scene-9c a touch more friction so the ideal/bridge-building path isn't the easiest emotionally as well as mechanically
Consider light foreshadowing or an easter egg for players who pick all-empathy or all-confidence paths (e.g., a unique Jordan line) to reward specific build consistency
Add minor callback dialogue referencing the hallway choice more directly in scene-4 or scene-6, not just in end-branch dialogue

summary.verdict: A well-crafted, emotionally resonant episode with strong choice architecture and a clear moral centerpiece; minor pacing and balance issues in the back half and around the 'punished' choice keep it from being best-in-class.
keyIssues: Choice-free tail section reduces agency in the final act
Scene-7c reads as narratively punished despite design intent of neutrality
Bridge-building path resolves too easily given its narrative importance
topStrengths: Genuinely meaningful moral choice at the group-project crisis point
Strong branch divergence across seating, lunch, and ending choices
Smart reincorporation of earlier choices via branchDialogue system
mustFix: Add agency/choice moment to the final act before the ending selection
Soften narrative judgment on the 'side with Mia' choice to match stated design philosophy
niceToHave: Small callback to hallway choice earlier in the episode
More friction in the bridge-building resolution
Unique flavor rewards for consistent trait builds across a full playthrough
